claim: For all integers n, n^2 + n is even.
For all integers n, n^3 - n is divisible by 6.
For all integers n, n^5 - n is divisible by 30.
For all integers n, n^7 - n is divisible by 42.
For all integers n, n^9 - n is divisible by 30.
For all odd integers n, n^2 - 1 is divisible by 8.
For all odd integers n, n^4 - 1 is divisible by 16.
For all odd integers n, n^8 - 1 is divisible by 32.
For all integers n not divisible by 3, n^2 - 1 is divisible by 3.
For all integers n not divisible by 5, n^4 - 1 is divisible by 5.
For all integers n not divisible by 2 or 3, n^2 - 1 is divisible by 24.
For all integers n not divisible by 2 or 5, n^4 - 1 is divisible by 80.
For all integers n, the greatest common divisor of n(n+1) and 2n+1 is 1.
For all integers n, if n is not a multiple of 3, then the greatest common divisor of n^3-n and 3 is 3.
For all integers n, if n^2+n is greater than 2, then the Euler totient of n^2+n is even.
For all non-zero integers n other than -1, the number of divisors of |n^2+n| is at least 2.
For all integers n, the product n(n+1)(n+2) is divisible by 6.
For all integers n, the product n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3) is divisible by 24.
For all integers n, the product n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)(n+4) is divisible by 120.
For all integers n, if |n| is prime and greater than 2, then the Euler totient of |n| is even.
For all integers n, if |n| is prime and greater than 3, then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 24.
For all integers n, if |n| is prime and greater than 5, then n^4 - 1 is divisible by 240.
For all integers n, if |n| is prime and greater than 7, then n^6 - 1 is divisible by 504.
For all positive integers n, if n is prime, then the sum of divisors of n is n + 1.
For all positive integers n, if n is prime, then the Euler totient of n is n - 1.
For all positive integers n, if n is prime, then the number of divisors of n is 2.
For all integers n greater than 1, the Euler totient of n is less than or equal to n - 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, the sum of divisors of n is greater than or equal to n + 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, the number of divisors of n is at least 2.
For all integers n greater than 1, the product of the Euler totient of n and the sum of divisors of n is at most n^2 - 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n is composite, then the product of the Euler totient of n and the sum of divisors of n is strictly less than n^2 - 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n is composite, then the number of divisors of n is strictly greater than 2.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n is composite, then the sum of divisors of n is strictly greater than n + 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n is composite, then the Euler totient of n is strictly less than n - 1.
For all positive integers n, the sum of the Euler totient function over all divisors of n equals n.
predicate: (n <= 0) or (sum(phi(d) for d in range(1, n + 1) if n % d == 0) == n)

claim: For all positive integers n, the number of divisors of n is at most 2 times the square root of n.
For all positive integers n, the sum of divisors of n is at most n times the number of divisors of n.
For all integers n greater than 2, the Euler totient of n is at least the square root of n / 2.
For all integers n greater than 1, n is prime if and only if the sum of divisors of n plus the Euler totient of n is 2n.
For all integers n greater than 1, n is prime if and only if the sum of divisors of n plus the Euler totient of n equals n times the number of divisors of n.
For all positive integers n, the number of divisors of n^2 is at most the square of the number of divisors of n.
For all positive integers n, the Euler totient of n^2 is equal to n times the Euler totient of n.
For all positive integers n, the sum of divisors of n^2 is at most the square of the sum of divisors of n.
For all positive integers n, the greatest common divisor of n and n+1 is 1.
For all positive integers n, the greatest common divisor of n and n+2 is at most 2.
For all positive integers n, the greatest common divisor of n^2 and (n+1)^2 is 1.
For all positive integers n, the greatest common divisor of n+1 and n^2+n+1 is 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, the greatest common divisor of n-1 and n^2+n+1 is either 1 or 3.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n is a multiple of 3, then the greatest common divisor of n-1 and n^2+n+1 is 1.
For all integers n greater than 1, if n leaves a remainder of 1 when divided by 3, then the greatest common divisor of n-1 and n^2+n+1 is 3.
For all positive integers n, the sum of divisors of n is odd if and only if n is a perfect square or twice a perfect square.
